data.title: US Treasury Adds Venmo for Debt Donations as Strategic Bitcoin Reserve Bill Stalls
data.description:
The US Treasury now accepts PayPal and Venmo for voluntary public debt contributions through its Pay.gov form. The update arrives as a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve bill targeting the same fiscal problem stalls in Congress.

Donations average roughly $120,000 a month against a $39 trillion total. Interest payments alone run near $88 billion a month, dwarfing any voluntary inflow.

A 64-Year-Old Program Meets Viral Attention

The “Gifts to Reduce the Public Debt” program has operated since 1961 under 31 U.S.C. § 3113. Treasury data show cumulative donations of about $67 million since 1996, with February 2026 inflows near $30,000.

Amid growing US debt. Senator Rand Paul has pushed his Six Penny Plan. The proposal would trim six cents from every federal dollar over five years.

“I introduced the Six Penny Plan because the answer to our debt crisis isn’t complicated. Cut six cents off every dollar. Balance the budget in five years. Protect your children’s future. The only thing standing in the way is Washington’s refusal to live within its means,” he stated.

Strategic Bitcoin Reserve as the alternative

Bitcoin advocates contrast the donation program with active proposals to build sovereign crypto holdings. The BITCOIN Act of 2025 was introduced by Senator Cynthia Lummis. It would direct the purchase of 1 million BTC over five years.

Asset manager VanEck has projected that a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve could trim US debt by 36% by 2050.

“Assuming today’s $900 trillion of total global financial assets compound at 7.0% from 2025 – 2049, Bitcoin would represent 18% of global financial assets in this scenario,” the firm added.

The bill remains stuck in committee. Lummis announced in December 2025 she will not seek reelection.

President Donald Trump’s executive order created the reserve on paper using forfeited coins. Operational deadlines have lapsed, and Congress has not appropriated new acquisition funds.

The companion Mined in America Act seeks to codify that framework.

The current outlook leaves taxpayers with two contrasting tools. Voluntary digital gifts sit on one side, while a stalled legislative push for fixed-supply reserves sits on the other.

data.title: California Church Affiliated With Suspected Shooter's Family Increases Security — WSJ
data.description:
By Joe Flint

TORRANCE, Calif.-On Sunday, there was a security and media presence at the Grace United Reform Church at Torrance, where the Allen family was affiliated. The church is in a nondescript office park, less than a mile away from the Allen home here.

A security guard, who declined to give his name, said to the small gathering of media that the church would have no statement. The church also requested that parishioners be left alone as they left services Sunday morning.

The security guard said he was called in on Saturday night to be at the church. A man with the same name as the father of Cole Allen, the 31-year-old Torrance man identified as the White House Correspondents' Dinner suspected shooter, was previously listed on the church's leadership page. The webpage returned a page-not-found error on Sunday morning. The security guard said he didn't know if that individual was affiliated with the church or present on the premises Sunday.

Gramercy Ave., near the Allen family home, was reopened that morning after being blocked off by law enforcement Saturday night.

A smaller media contingent remained across the street from the Allen house. FBI agents were in the neighborhood early Sunday and visited some neighboring houses, according to residents.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Changan Group Unveils Updated "Vast Ocean Plan 2.0" and Highlight Next-Generation BlueCore Hybrid at Auto China 2026
data.description:
Changan Group/ Key word(s): Product Launch

Changan Group Unveils Updated "Vast Ocean Plan 2.0" and Highlight Next-Generation BlueCore Hybrid at Auto China 2026

26.04.2026 / 21:15 CET/CEST

The issuer is solely responsible for the content of this announcement.

Building on Changan Group's long-term "1+4+4+5" strategy, the company is advancing global expansion through seven operational upgrades and next-generation hybrid technology.

BEIJING, April 26, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Changan Group officially launched the upgraded Vast Ocean Plan 2.0 at Auto China 2026, marking a new phase of its global expansion and reaffirming its long-term commitment to international markets. The Group also debuted BlueCore Hybrid versions of the fourth-generation EADO and CS75 PLUS, bringing its latest hybrid technology to two flagship models.

The announcement is built on Changan's 1+4+4+5 strategy, which prioritizes globalization as a core long-term transformation and sets five doubling goals by 2030, including overseas vehicle sales.

"Today, we are launching Vast Ocean Plan 2.0 with greater determination and a more open mindset," said Zhao Fei, General Manager of Changan Group "We will continue to follow our core principles—long-term development, localization, systematization, and responsible ESG practices—while evolving from an export-driven model to an integrated global operation spanning manufacturing, trade, investment, services, and sustainability."

A Stronger Global Roadmap

In 2025, Changan achieved 637,000 overseas sales, a year-on-year increase of 18.9%. It expanded into 21 new markets, launched 12 new models, and now operates in 118 countries and regions with 1,124 sales outlets. The Group runs 22 overseas manufacturing bases with an annual capacity of 350,000 units, and its global product portfolio reached 41 models, including 25 fuel vehicles and 16 new energy vehicles.

Vast Ocean Plan 2.0 transforms Changan's globalization from "going global" to "taking root locally". By 2030, the Group targets 1.5 million overseas vehicle sales, with an ambitious goal of 1.8 million. Regionally, it aims to lead among Chinese brands in Europe, rank top 5 global brands in Eurasia, and enter top 10 (with top 5 ambition) in Southeast Asia, Middle East & Africa, and Central & South America.

Updated Vast Ocean Plan: Seven Strategic Upgrades

To achieve these targets, the plan focuses on seven strategic upgrades: technology, product, brand, partnership model, investment, service and team. These upgrades will strengthen global competitiveness, support the five doubling goals, and bring high-quality mobility experiences to global users.

Advancements in Hybrid Technologies and Unmanned Logistics

Alongside the strategic upgrade, Changan displayed cutting-edge technologies and new products. CHANGAN KAICHENG launched Robovan unmanned logistics vehicle based on SDA architecture, equipped with multi-layer redundancy systems for safe last-mile delivery, and has reached in-depth cooperation with JD Logistics for batch delivery.

Changan's BlueCore Hybrid, built on iDE-H deep electrification intelligent hybrid architecture, integrates three core components and an intelligent control system, balancing fuel efficiency, performance and quietness. The fourth-generation EADO BlueCore Hybrid hits a minimum tested fuel consumption of 1.6L/100km, daily average of 3.87L/100km and a 1,500km full-tank range. Backed by 1,500 core patents, it has completed 20,000 hours of bench tests and 2 million kilometers of road trials. The fourth-generation CS75 PLUS BlueCore Hybrid features around 3L/100km fuel consumption and segment-exclusive CDC suspension for better comfort and stability.

In addition, AVATR's VISION XPECTRA made its Asian debut, with AVATR 12 and 06T also unveiled; DEEPAL showcased six models including the newly launched L06 REEV.

A New Chapter in Global Expansion

With Vast Ocean Plan 2.0, Changan will scale its global footprint and enhance capabilities in technology, products, brand and operations. Powered by next-generation hybrid and global models, the Group will bring Chinese automotive innovation to more users and create long-term value for customers worldwide.

data.title: Congress Requests Briefings From Secret Service — WSJ
data.description:
By Natalie Andrews

House Oversight Committee Chairman James Comer requested a briefing on security from the U.S. Secret Service. Comer also held hearings in 2024 when Trump was shot in Butler, Pa., during a campaign rally.

Comer also called on Congress to fully fund the Department of Homeland Security, which has been in a tenuous position since the beginning of February, when appropriations lapsed. The administration has used some emergency funds and Congressional spending from the tax bill to pay officers and Transportation Security Administration workers, but many employees are still unpaid.

"DHS, including Secret Service, has been unfunded for more than 70 days now," Comer said on social media. "It's time to stop the games & fund DHS."

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Rattled Reporters Trade Stories Over Spritzes — WSJ
data.description:
By Joshua Jamerson

Guests at a Sunday brunch hosted by Politico and the Star, formerly known as NOTUS, traded stories from the night before, including how they ducked and covered under tables and which cabinet secretaries they got a glimpse of being evacuated by secret service. Attendees at the annual brunch, hosted at the sprawling Georgetown estate of Robert Allbritton, sipped espresso martinis, Aperol spritzes and champagne. A carver shaved jamón near the bar, and by the pool, empanadas were served.

Some were overheard musing about whether the White House Correspondents' Dinner would follow through on rescheduling the event. And if so, whether they needed to order new gowns or if it would be socially acceptable to repeat their outfits from Saturday.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Lionsgate's 'Michael' Wins Weekend Box Office in Fresh Record for Biopics — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Janet H. Cho

Lionsgate's biopic Michael danced its way to the top of the domestic box office despite some critics' mixed reviews.

The two-hour film, also from Comcast's Universal Pictures, explores Michael Jackson's childhood and his transformation into the King of Pop. It delivered an estimated $97 million in domestic ticket sales in its debut weekend, including $39.5 million on opening day, according to Comscore.

Michael, starring Jackson's nephew Jaafar Jackson and Colman Domingo as Joe Jackson, now ranks as the highest-grossing biopic of all time. It's the year's second-biggest domestic opening after Universal Pictures and Nintendo 's The Super Mario Galaxy Movie, which grossed $131.7 million in domestic ticket sales over the three-day Easter weekend earlier this month.

Michael grossed a projected $217.4 million worldwide through Sunday, including $24.4 million on IMAX screens, the biggest ever IMAX opening for a musical biopic.

Michael helped boost the weekend's domestic box office for Hollywood to an estimated $152.5 million. So far this year, domestic ticket sales of $2.57 billion are 14.2% above this point in 2025, according to Comscore.

"The movie is a hit with moviegoers around the globe who just wanna be startin' somethin' while having a fun, escapist experience with other MJ fans," Comscore's head of marketplace trends Paul Dergarabedian said. "Though critical reviews were not generally positive, the movie is truly critic-proof and by staying away from the more controversial elements of Jackson's life, Michael is just a fun escapist romp and that's exactly what audiences were looking for at the multiplex this weekend."

The Wall Street Journal said Michael is projected to gross more than $500 million worldwide, and that footage for a potential sequel has already been shot.

Dergarabedian said the opening of one of the year's most highly anticipated movies was "perfectly positioned ahead of the start of the summer movie season." Walt Disney's The Devil Wears Prada 2 is scheduled to debut on Friday and "poised to outpace even the most generous opening weekend projections," he added.

The Disney sequel to the 2006 original reunites Anne Hathaway, Meryl Streep, and Emily Blunt. The Devil Wears Prada grossed more than $326.6 million worldwide, according to BoxOfficeMojo.

Super Mario Galaxy is still soaring at the cinema, adding another $21.2 million to take second place at the weekend domestic box office in its fourth weekend. The animated movie has sold $386.5 million in North America since opening on April 1, according to Comscore. It has grossed $831.5 million worldwide, including $37.3 million in IMAX.

In third place this weekend, Amazon MGM Studios' space adventure Project Hail Mary sold another $13.2 million, bringing its total domestic sales to $305.4 million. It has sold $613.3 million in global ticket sales to date, including $92.4 million in IMAX.

Elsewhere in Hollywood, more than 90% of the members of the Writers Guild of America West and the Writers Guild of America East unions voted to ratify their new four-year contract with the Alliance of Motion Picture and Television Producers. The contract includes an increase in health contributions, pay increases, higher residuals when work re-airs, higher viewership-based streaming bonuses, and other agreements.

Dergarabedian called it an important development, noting that previous writers' strikes have "profoundly impacted production schedules and the release slate, particularly for theatrical films which can then have a truly negative effect on the box office bottom line. This is a win for the entire industry, small screens, and big screens alike."

The actors' union SAG-AFTRA is expected to resume negotiations on its new contract on Monday. The Directors Guild begins its contract talks on May 11.

Write to Janet H. Cho at janet.cho@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Litecoin gives post-attack update, but other devs doubt zero-day theory
data.description:
Litecoin, a layer-1 proof-of-work (PoW) blockchain network, was hit by a “zero-day” vulnerability on Saturday, which caused a 13-block reorganization of the chain, according to the Litecoin team, while other developers say the bug may have been previously known.

The bug caused a denial of service (DoS) attack on mining pools with newly updated software, suppressing their hashing power, according to an update from the Litecoin team.

This allowed older nodes to “peg out” coins to decentralized exchanges and cross-chain swap protocols, resulting in invalid transactions being posted to the network’s MimbleWimble Extension Blocks (MWEB) privacy layer, according to the team.

The updated nodes eventually regained control of the network’s hashing power, executing a 13-block reorganization that reversed the invalid transactions, which will not appear on the main chain. The bug has now been fully patched, the Litecoin team said.

The incident comes amid an uptick in zero-day vulnerabilities -- that is, potential code exploits that are unknown to the software developers launching the product -- as AI systems like Anthropic’s Claude Mythos outperform human beings at identifying such attack surfaces.

Some may have had advanced knowledge of software bug

A Binance address funded the attacker earlier in the week, suggesting that they had pre-planned the attack and had knowledge of the code exploit beforehand, according to Alex Shevchenko, co-founder of Aurora, a layer-2 scaling network. He said:

“The fact that protocol automatically handled the reorg once DoS stopped, which is great, means that some portion of the hashrate was actually running an updated code. Thus, this bug was known, and it's not a zero-day.”

“The timing and targeting suggest this wasn't a random opportunity,” blockchain developer Vadim said in response, adding, “Low hashrate layer 1’s are not safe collateral for cross-chain value anymore.”

Cross-chain bridges, which transfer digital assets between different blockchain protocols, have long been a major attack surface in crypto, causing billions of dollars in losses over the years.

The most recent high-profile example of a bridge exploit was the April 18 attack on the Kelp restaking protocol, which earlier this month left the platform drained of about $293 million.

data.title: Risk-On Surge Lifts Crypto Outlook as Equity ETFs Hit Record $7.5 Billion Daily Pace
data.description:
US equity ETFs (exchange-traded funds) drew a record $7.5 billion in average daily inflows during April. The pace more than doubles the $2.9 billion March average, according to Strategas Asset Management.

The data shows cumulative inflows above $100 billion since the March 30 low. The reading reflects institutional risk appetite that historically supports crypto ETF flows and tokenized asset adoption.

Equity ETF Demand Reflects Post-March Risk-On Rotation

Strategas figures show April 2026 daily flows averaged $7,474 million versus $2,950 million in March. Aggregate US-listed ETF inflows across all categories now track near $524 billion year-to-date through mid-April.

The acceleration follows a March pullback driven by tariff-related volatility. April flows run more than double the 2025 daily average of $3.7 billion. The shift signals a sharp sentiment reversal among allocators.

“Investors are pouring more capital into equity funds than ever,” wrote analysts at the Kobeissi Letter.

Outflows from active mutual funds and modest reductions in money market balances have funded part of the surge.

Fixed income ETFs continue attracting capital alongside equities, indicating broader deployment rather than rotation.

Follow us on X to get the latest news as it happens

Same Capital Pools Drive Bitcoin and Ethereum ETF Demand

Spot Bitcoin and Ethereum ETF products have rebounded alongside equities since the March bottom.

BlackRock’s iShares Bitcoin Trust IBIT holds cumulative lifetime flows above $63 billion. The fund draws the same allocator base that rotated into broad equity indices.

BlackRock’s Ethereum ETF has shown similar institutional interest in recent weeks. Large managers including Apollo and Hamilton Lane have begun directing 1% to 2% of portfolio allocations toward digital assets.

The convergence is also visible on the TradFi side. Pensions, endowments, and family offices increasingly treat regulated crypto ETFs as portfolio components similar to broad equity trackers.

“Banks. Pensions. Insurers. Asset managers. 79% plan to invest in digital assets. Over 50% allocating within one year. Most targeting 2–5% allocations. Trillions are coming…..,” remarked TradFi researcher Ryan Solomon.

Tokenization Brings Equity Demand On-Chain

Strong appetite for accessible equity exposure has reinforced the case for tokenization of real-world assets (RWAs). Total tokenized RWA value sits above $30 billion, with US Treasuries, private credit, and equities forming leading categories.

Platforms including Kraken xStocks, Ondo Global Markets, and Backed Finance already offer tokenized US equities and ETF exposure.

Issuers such as BlackRock, Fidelity, and JPMorgan continue advancing tokenization pilots tied to settlement and custody.

Risks remain. Procyclical flows can reverse if macro conditions sour. Concentration in mega-cap technology still drives index performance.

“A key implication is that macro now has to be filtered through flows. If hedge funds are running unusually low net exposure and retail flips from loading puts to chasing calls, price can overshoot well beyond what underlying growth, earnings, or valuation would normally justify. Markets are increasingly driven by flows and market structure idiosyncrasies that makes timing harder, not easier,” noted analysts at Forward Guidance.

Operators in custody, prime brokerage, and on-chain settlement face a key question. Can record equity demand translate into hybrid TradFi-crypto products in coming quarters?

Subscribe to our YouTube channel to watch leaders and journalists provide expert insights

data.title: Safety. For Family: TIGGO9 Completes Public Three-Vehicle Composite Crash Verification During the 2026 International Business Summit
data.description:
Chery Automobile Co., Ltd./ Key word(s): Miscellaneous

Safety. For Family: TIGGO9 Completes Public Three-Vehicle Composite Crash Verification During the 2026 International Business Summit

26.04.2026 / 20:15 CET/CEST

The issuer is solely responsible for the content of this announcement.

WUHU, China, April 26, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- In April 2026, during the 2026 International Business Summit, CHERY completed a public three-vehicle composite crash validation of the TIGGO9 at the Chery Crash Safety Laboratory, witnessed on site by global dealers, international media and overseas user representatives. Designed around real-world high-risk traffic scenarios, the test used a composite crash setup to assess the safety performance of CHERY's flagship SUV and further demonstrate the brand's safety philosophy: Safety. For Family.

The test simulated a severe front-and-rear impact scenario. The TIGGO9, as the core test vehicle, sustained a 50 km/h frontal impact from a TIGGO7 while being simultaneously rear-ended by another vehicle at 40 km/h, placing its body structure, restraint systems and post-collision emergency response under comprehensive evaluation. After the crash, the passenger compartment remained intact, with no obvious deformation to the pillars and sufficient survival space preserved. The airbags and side curtain airbags deployed properly, the seat belt systems functioned effectively, the doors unlocked automatically and could be opened normally, the fuel system showed no leakage, and the hazard warning lights operated as intended.

This performance is supported by the TIGGO9's systematic safety design, including a high-rigidity body structure with 85% high-strength steel and 21% hot-stamped steel, an optimized load-transfer path, and a 10-airbag system. With multiple global five-star safety certifications already earned, CHERY continues to strengthen its safety credentials through real-world validation. Looking ahead, CHERY will continue to raise safety standards and deliver reliable protection for families around the world.

data.title: Netanyahu's biggest rivals join forces for Israel's next election
data.description:
Key points:
  • Former PM Bennett, opposition leader Lapid merge parties
  • The two ousted Netanyahu in 2021 election
  • Latest polls predict a Netanyahu loss in upcoming vote

By Maayan Lubell

Two of Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's most formidable political rivals said on Sunday they were joining forces in a bid to oust his coalition government in the upcoming election expected later this year.

The former prime ministers - right-wing Naftali Bennett and centrist Yair Lapid - issued statements announcing the merger of their parties, Bennett 2026 and There is a Future.

"We are standing here together for the sake of our children. The State of Israel must change direction," Lapid said standing alongside Bennett at a joint news conference.

Bennett said the new party will be called Together, and that he will be its leader. "After 30 years it is time to part with Netanyahu and open a new chapter for Israel," he said.

Since his first term in the 1990s, Netanyahu has become a polarising figure at home and abroad.

JOINING FORCES ONCE MORE

Bennett and Lapid have joined forces before, putting an end to Netanyahu's successive 12-year tenure in a 2021 election, only to form a coalition government that with a thin majority and deeply divided over major issues like the Israeli-Palestinian conflict, survived barely 18 months.

Their coalition included for the first time in Israel's history a party drawn from the country's Arab minority - Palestinian by heritage, Israeli by citizenship - the United Arab List (UAL).

Before that the duo muscled their way into his 2013 coalition government in a move that left Netanyahu's traditional ultra-Orthodox Jewish allies out.

Netanyahu, Israel's longest-serving prime minister, made a comeback when he won the November 2022 election and formed the most right-wing government in Israel's history.

But Hamas' 2023 attack on southern Israel, which plunged the Middle East into turmoil and saw Israel fighting on multiple fronts, left Netanyahu's security credentials in tatters and polls since then have predicted that he will lose the next election, due by the end of October.

Netanyahu, the most dominant Israeli politician of his generation, has shown remarkable political survival skills in the past, however.

On Sunday, he posted a 2021 photo of Bennett and Lapid with UAL head Mansour Abbas. "They did it once, they'll do it again," Netanyahu's Telegram post said, an apparent swipe at their short-lived 2021 coalition that included UAL.

Bennett said that he will not seek a coalition with Arab parties again and ruled out ceding any land to enemies, an apparent reference to the Palestinians' goal of establishing an independent state in territories occupied by Israel.

SHIFTING POLITICAL MAP

Bennett, 54, a pugnacious former army commando turned tech millionaire, has been trailing Netanyahu in election polls. An April 23 survey by Israel's N12 News found Bennett securing 21 of the Knesset's 120 seats, against 25 seats for Netanyahu's Likud.

It found Lapid's party securing only seven seats, down from the 24 it currently holds, but with Netanyahu's coalition of right-wing and religious parties commanding only 50 seats, against at least 60 seats for Bennett and Lapid's likely coalition that would include several smaller factions.

The survey was on par with previous polls by academic institutions and other Israeli media, which have put Bennett as the top contender against Netanyahu, though the political map could still shift and change.

Lapid, 62, a telegenic former TV news anchor who writes pop songs and thrillers, speaks as the voice of Israel's secular middle class, which has become increasingly incensed by what it sees as an unfair tax and military service burden.

Netanyahu's ultra-religious political allies have been seeking an exemption for their communities - who have low employment and many state benefits - from the conscript military.

It is a hot-button issue in Israel that has become all the more pressing since the military has warned of being over-stretched and with the last two years exacting the highest military death toll in decades.

Both Lapid and Bennett have made it a central issue for their campaign. They have also criticised Netanyahu for failing to leverage military gains into strategic wins over Iran and the groups it supports in Lebanon and Gaza - Hezbollah and Hamas.

data.title: JPMorgan Sees Tokenized ETFs As The Next Big Thing, But Adoption Timeline Is Still Unclear
data.description:
Key points:
  • JPMorgan’s Ciarán Fitzpatrick said tokenization could reshape exchange-traded funds, enabling near-instant settlement and 24/7 access.
  • He cautioned that meaningful “good use cases” for tokenized ETFs are still likely a few years away despite ongoing experimentation.
  • The US Securities and Exchange Commission’s Hester Peirce recently urged firms exploring tokenized financial products to engage directly with regulators as the space evolves.

Tokenized exchange-traded funds (ETFs) could play a major role in reshaping financial markets, according to JPMorgan, even as meaningful real-world applications remain some time away.

“We believe tokenization will certainly drive how the market changes, not just for ETFs but across the funds industry as a whole,” said Ciarán Fitzpatrick, JPMorgan’s global head of ETF product, securities services, according to a report released on Friday.

Fitzpatrick said tokenization could enhance core ETF functions such as creation and redemption, while enabling “near-instant settlement” and continuous, round-the-clock market access. However, he cautioned that the industry was still “a couple of years away from some good use cases.”

Tokenization is the process of representing traditional financial assets on blockchain networks. In the case of ETFs, this could take the form of digital tokens that mirror fund shares or are issued directly on-chain, potentially reducing operational friction and improving efficiency.

JPMorgan Expands Crypto Exposure Via BlackRock

JPMorgan is already exploring such applications through Kinexys, its blockchain-focused business unit, as part of broader industry-wide experimentation. While J.P. Morgan does not have its own Bitcoin ETF, its Self-Directed Investing allows clients to buy and sell third-party cryptocurrency ETFs. The bank has also increased its holdings in spot Bitcoin, BlackRock’s iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT), and the Ethereum (ETH) ETF.

BlackRock’s IBIT’s price was down by 0.24% during after-hours trading. On Friday, it closed over $44. On Stocktwits, the retail sentiment around IBIT remained in the ‘extremely bullish’ zone, while chatter around it stayed ‘high’ over the past day.

Regulatory Momentum Builds Around Tokenization

The push toward tokenization comes as both financial institutions and regulators show increasing openness to blockchain-based financial products.

Speaking in an interview with CNBC’s The Exchange last month, Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) Commissioner Hester Peirce said the agency wanted companies experimenting with new financial products, including tokenized instruments, to engage directly with regulators as the market matures.

The SEC has also supported early efforts related to tokenized securities, including approving rule changes that enable platforms like Nasdaq to facilitate trading of tokenized shares.

Major firms, including the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE), Robinhood (HOOD), Kraken (KRAKEN), and Coinbase (COIN), are exploring ways to scale tokenized equity offerings.

Despite the momentum, tokenized ETFs remain in an early phase. Analysts broadly expect tokenized assets to grow significantly over the coming years, with projections ranging from around $2 trillion to more than $10 trillion by 2030, according to JPMorgan’s report.

data.title: XRP Ready For Next Bull Run? Here’s How This Analyst Arrived At $13 Target
data.description:
After enjoying a modest recovery in the early weeks of April, the price of XRP appears to have settled around the $1.4 level. Interestingly, a popular analyst on the social media platform X has put forward an audacious target for the altcoin, projecting its price to reach $13 in the next cycle. However, the path to this lofty target is not as straightforward as it appears, as an initial price drop for XRP could be potentially imminent.

Price Could Fall To $0.9 Before Next Bull Run: Analyst

In an April 25th post on the X platform, crypto analyst Ali Martinez postulated that the price of XRP could reach as high as $13 in the next bull run. However, the market pundit also noted that the cryptocurrency might need to drop below the $1 mark before this significant upward move materializes.

This price outlook is based on the appearance of an ascending triangle pattern on the XRP monthly chart. The ascending triangle is a technical analysis pattern characterized by an inverse right-angled triangle with a horizontal upper boundary (typically resistance) and a diagonal rising lower trendline (connecting the higher lows).

An ascending triangle formation is usually considered a pause in a trend, with prices often breaking out to the original upward trend. However, an ascending triangle formation can act as a trend-reversal pattern and a bearish signal when the asset’s price breaks below the lower trendline, thereby negating the initial uptrend.

As shown in the chart above, the ascending triangle captures XRP’s price action over the past few years, with the upper boundary (around $3.32) resisting further upward movement back to early 2025. The altcoin’s price has since been in a downward trend, with Martinez projecting a potential bear-market bottom around the lower diagonal trendline (around $0.9).

If the XRP price does bottom at $0.9, Martinez expects that the altcoin will travel to as high as $13 in the next bull run. However, a conservative target for investors could be around $3.32, which is the token’s high from the previous cycle.

It is worth noting that if the XRP price succumbs to further bearish pressure and breaks the support around $0.9, it could fall to as low as $0.11, where the next significant support zone lies.

XRP Price At A Glance

As of this writing, the price of XRP is around $1.43, down 0.5% over the past 24 hours. According to data from CoinGecko, the altcoin has jumped by more than 6% over the past month, reflecting an improving crypto market climate in the second quarter of 2026.

data.title: Oz Pearlman Was 'Eye-to-Eye' With Trump on Stage: 'Are We About to Die?' — WSJ
data.description:
By Philip Wegmann

One moment, Oz Pearlman was "schmoozing" President Trump and the first lady at the White House Correspondent's Dinner, guessing the name of White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt's expected baby, he told The Wall Street Journal.

The next, the celebrity mentalist, slated to be the entertainment that evening, was taking cover at the direction of law-enforcement.

Pearlman recalled turning his head to see Trump surrounded by members of the Secret Service less than a foot away. "I have a photo of it in my mind," he said, "just full eye contact." The mentalist said that he remembers thinking, "'Oh no, are we about to die?'"

"I thought there was about to be a bomb explosion," he said. "One hundred percent that was my gut reaction."

What did Pearlman read in the face of the president? "He was processing what was going on in that moment, because I think he saw something," the mentalist said. "And before he could even really figure it out-that's the speed of the Secret Service-he was already out." The performer then "army-crawled" off the dais himself.

Pearlman said he saw "organized chaos" backstage, law enforcement with "guns in hand," and finally "a very calm and composed" Vice President JD Vance. "He came over, and I recall him saying, "the president is OK-he's coming back." Trump had wanted to continue the night as scheduled but instead returned to the White House at the advice of the Secret Service.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Fed Rate Decision, Verizon, Coca-Cola, Apple, Meta, Exxon, and Much More to Watch This Week — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Dan Lam

Equities continued their push higher last week, with the Nasdaq Composite and S&P 500 indexes both closing at record highs, up 1.5% and 0.5% respectively. The fragile cease-fire between the U.S. and Iran is holding for now.

In such an environment, Wall Street can focus on strong earnings, especially from semiconductor companies, which have propelled the PHLX Semiconductor Index to an amazing 18-day winning streak and record high.

Investors will get a plethora of earnings this week, as about one-third of S&P 500 companies will report results, including five of the Magnificent Seven. Verizon Communications will release earnings on Monday, followed by Coca-Cola, T-Mobile US, and Visa on Tuesday. AbbVie, Alphabet, Amazon.com, Meta Platforms, Microsoft, and Qualcomm will announce quarterly results on Wednesday, while Apple, Caterpillar, Eli Lilly, Mastercard, and Merck will do so on Thursday. Chevron and Exxon Mobil will report their earnings on Friday.

The week also brings the Federal Open Market Committee's monetary-policy decision on Wednesday, and potentially Jerome Powell's last press conference as Fed chair. The FOMC will most likely keep interest rates unchanged, but more important is clues as to how willing the central bank be to will look past rising inflation, mostly from energy prices and the knock-on effects.

The main economic data of the week will be the personal consumption expenditures price index, to be released by the Bureau of Economic Analysis on Thursday. The consumer price index rose 3.3% year over year in March, and the PCE price index is expected to show a similar jump. It's been more than five years since inflation has been at or below the Federal Reserve's 2% target.

data.title: Trump Says Iran Can ‘Call Us’, Predicts War Ending Soon: Report
data.description:
Key points:
  • President Donald Trump told Fox News that the Iran war may end "very soon," adding that conversations will take place over the phone.
  • Trump also stated that any agreement will focus on Iran's nuclear capabilities.
  • The statements followed a WHCA dinner shooting that resulted in a suspect's arrest, but no Iranian connection.

President Donald Trump reportedly said on Sunday the Iran war could end “very soon,” signaling openness to talks even as tensions remain high after a shooting at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner.

Reports also stated that talks with Iran might be held remotely. Trump allegedly told Fox News, saying, “We’re going to do it by phone.” Trump added that he thinks the “Iran war will come to an end very soon, and we will be victorious,” he said, adding that some colleagues were “very reasonable” and some were not.

The president also chastised international allies, stating it had volunteered to send ships only after the war ended, "and that's not good," and said NATO "was not there for us on Iran." He spoke calmly about China, stating that Beijing "could have been much worse than they could have been," that he was "not overly disappointed," but that it "could help more."

This follows President Donald Trump's announcement that a "shooter has been apprehended" during the evacuation of him and other attendees from the White House Correspondents' Association (WHCA) dinner on Saturday.

Trump called the gunman a "lone wolf" and claimed he was "taken down by some very brave members of the Secret Service" during a White House press conference. Cole Tomas Allen, 31, of Torrance, California, was identified by authorities as the suspect.

data.title: Standex: The Electronics Company the Market Still Reads as an Industrial
data.description:
What the Market Has Not Updated

For most of its history, Standex International was a reliable but unremarkable multi-industry manufacturer, the kind of business that analysts filed under diversified industrials and revisited occasionally, usually to note that margins were acceptable and end-market exposure was broad. That characterization was accurate enough for a company selling engraving tools, food service equipment, hydraulic cylinders, and reed switches to whoever needed them. It is no longer accurate, and the gap between what the business has become and how the market continues to price it is the animating question for anyone evaluating the stock today.

The transformation is not a story management is telling. It is visible in the filings. Electronics, the segment built on reed switches and magnetic sensors that once sat comfortably alongside Engraving and Specialty Solutions as one of five roughly equivalent revenue contributors, now represents 52% of sales and 63% of adjusted operating income. The adjustment did not happen through organic drift. It happened through a combination of deliberate portfolio pruning, years of exiting lower-margin, lower-growth businesses, and the October 2024 acquisition of Amran Instrument Transformers and Narayan Powertech, a $462 million transaction that was the largest in the company's history and that doubled its exposure to what management calls fast-growth markets: electrical grid modernization, defense electronics, space commercialization, and electric vehicles.

These are not incidental markets. Electrical grid modernization is a multi-decade spending cycle driven by three converging forces: aging infrastructure across developed economies, rising demand from data centers supporting AI infrastructure, and the need for transmission capacity to support renewable energy integration. Standex, through the Amran and Narayan businesses, is a custom-component supplier to the major OEMs in that market, Eaton, GE, Schneider Electric, providing the instrument transformers that enable accurate measurement, protection, and control of grid networks. This is not a commodity position. The products are engineered specifically for individual customer applications, specified by engineers, and unlikely to be switched after qualification. The relationship model is closer to an embedded partner than a vendor.

The stock has re-rated materially over the past twelve months, and the direction of that movement is correct. A business whose dominant segment was growing at double-digit organic rates and earning adjusted operating margins approaching 20% at the company level should not have been priced where it was. The re-rating acknowledges that.

What it does not yet reflect is a more precise question: what should the Electronics segment be worth on its own terms? Backed out of the company-level enterprise value, the implied EBITDA multiple being applied to a business generating adjusted operating margins above 28% on grid, defense, and space components sits near 20 to 22 times. AMETEK earns lower margins, grows more slowly, and trades at 22 times. Curtiss-Wright's defense and power segments trade at 33 times. The premium attached to those businesses for owning exactly the same economics, custom-engineered components, long qualification cycles, specification-driven customer relationships,has not found its way into the implied valuation of this segment yet.

It has not.

Anatomy of Resilience

Standex operates across five reporting segments, but the investment story concentrates in one of them with unusual clarity. The other four segments, Engraving, Engineering Technologies, Scientific, and Specialty Solutions, represent roughly half of revenue but collectively generate less than 40% of adjusted operating income.

Engraving, contributing approximately 16% of sales, provides mold texturizing and tooling for automotive and industrial customers. It is a slow-growth cash generator whose organic revenue growth is constrained by the pace of new automotive platform launches. Engineering Technologies, at roughly 14% of sales, produces near-net formed components for aerospace and defense applications under the Spincraft and McStarlite brands, with revenue tied directly to Boeing and Airbus production cycles. Scientific, a high-margin niche business generating adjusted operating margins above 24%, ran into a real but temporary headwind as NIH funding cuts deferred purchasing from research institutions. Specialty Solutions, the smallest segment at roughly 9% of sales, is the one management has most clearly identified as non-core; the divestiture of Federal Industries in early 2026 is the clearest signal of where this segment is heading.

Electronics is built around three product families. Switches and sensors, principally reed switches and reed relays, serve HVAC, industrial automation, and security applications, growing at mid-to-upper single-digit organic rates and generating steady cash flow. Magnetics, covering wound transformers, inductors, and power conversion components, serves power supply and energy conversion applications. The third and most consequential family is the Grid business, centered on the Amran and Narayan platform, which manufactures current and voltage instrument transformers for low and medium voltage electrical grid equipment. Grid business sales have grown roughly 35% since the acquisition closed, and management's long-term target of over $340 million in fast-growth market sales by fiscal 2028 is predominantly this story.

The business Amran and Narayan brought is structurally attractive in ways that are not immediately obvious from the product description. Instrument transformers are the measuring and sensing elements inside switchgear and protection systems; they tell grid operators exactly what is happening in a circuit at any moment, enabling protection relays to respond correctly when something goes wrong. Amran and Narayan entered the Standex portfolio with an adjusted EBITDA margin above 40%, against a company-wide average in the high teens. The products are custom-engineered, the qualification processes are long, and the switching costs for grid OEMs are high. A utility commissioning a substation in 2026 is specifying components that will remain in service for twenty to thirty years. The choice of instrument transformer supplier is not revisited casually.

What makes the comparison with ITT, AMETEK, and Curtiss-Wright illuminating rather than merely convenient is that all three are businesses whose dominant revenue streams come from engineered components sold into long-cycle, specification-driven markets. The economics of customer intimacy, long replacement cycles, and high switching costs are not unique to Standex. But Standex's Electronics segment is currently generating those economics at adjusted operating margins above 28%, comparable to or better than what AMETEK and ITT report for their most premium segments, while the company-level multiple still reflects the lower-margin businesses sitting alongside it in the portfolio.

The more pointed question is why a reader drawn to the electrical grid modernization theme would select a component supplier over the system integrators, ABB, Eaton, Schneider Electric, that sell the complete switchgear and protection systems into which Standex's instrument transformers are installed. The answer begins with an observation: those companies are not alternatives to this position. They are the customers. Standex does not compete with ABB for a grid contract. It supplies ABB with the current and voltage measurement elements that enable ABB's own protection systems to function correctly. That positions the Grid business at the most defensible layer of the value chain, the custom-engineered, application-specific component that a system integrator cannot profitably internalize, without the commodity automation and lower-margin industrial work that sit alongside ABB's and Eaton's high-value segments. For an investor seeking concentrated exposure to grid modernization at the component economics rather than the system level, ABB and Eaton represent the market being addressed. Standex is the pick-and-shovel inside it.

The Inflection in the Numbers

The financial trajectory of the past eighteen months tells a story that the company's headline earnings multiple has not yet absorbed. In fiscal 2022, Standex generated $663 million in revenue at an adjusted operating margin of 15.4%. In the trailing twelve months ended December 31, 2025, the same company generated $868.5 million in revenue at an adjusted operating margin approaching 20%, with the Electronics segment alone running above 28%. That is not incremental improvement. It is a fundamentally different earnings profile attached to a multiple that has not fully repriced for it.

The most recent earnings release, covering the second fiscal quarter of 2026 ended December 31, 2025, crystallized the inflection. Total revenue reached $221.3 million, a 16.6% increase year-on-year, with 6.4% of that coming from organic sources. The Electronics segment posted $115.7 million in revenue, up 20.6% year-on-year, with organic growth of 11.1% and a book-to-bill ratio of 1.08, its sixth consecutive quarter at or near 1.0 or above. Adjusted operating margin for the company reached 19.0%, up 30 basis points year-on-year. Free cash flow was $13.0 million in the quarter, compared to $2.2 million in the same period a year earlier.

The new product engine deserves direct attention because it is the mechanism through which organic growth compounds independently of acquisition-driven scale. Standex released 16 new products in fiscal 2025, generating $55 million in new product sales against $38 million the prior year. In fiscal 2026, the company is targeting more than 15 additional launches expected to contribute approximately 300 basis points of incremental revenue growth. This is not a normal industrial product development cycle, it is a structured, measured program that management tracks quarterly and that is producing compounding results. New product sales in the second fiscal quarter of 2026 grew 13% year-on-year and contributed 7% to total revenue.

Fast-growth market sales totaled approximately $61 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 and represented 28% of total company revenue. For fiscal year 2026, management has reaffirmed guidance for fast-growth market sales to exceed $270 million, a greater than 45% increase year-on-year. More than half of that figure is attributable to grid solutions, with the remainder distributed across defense, space, renewable energy, and electric vehicles. Each of these sub-markets is structurally independent of the others, which means the revenue base has a degree of diversification within the fast-growth category that is not always fully appreciated from the outside.

The leverage trajectory is the remaining variable for an owner focused on balance sheet normalization. Net debt stood at $437.7 million at December 31, 2025, representing a ratio of 2.3 times adjusted EBITDA on the most recent trailing basis. Standex paid down approximately $10 million of debt during the second fiscal quarter and is committed to further reduction through fiscal 2026, with management guidance for interest expense of $7 to $7.5 million in the third quarter. Capital expenditures are guided at $33 to $38 million for the full fiscal year, reflecting deliberate investment in capacity expansion, a Croatia greenfield facility, additional manufacturing in Mexico and Houston, to support the Grid growth trajectory. These are not defensive expenditures. They are pre-investment in a backlog that management is already contracted to serve.

The five-year average ROIC of approximately 12.8% is the anchor that puts the current acquisition-suppressed figure of 6.5% in context. That historical ROIC reflects what the business earns on capital when the balance sheet is not carrying a large, recently closed transaction. The acquisition of Amran and Narayan was immediately accretive to revenue, margins, and earnings per share, as disclosed at announcement, but it also added over $460 million to the capital base. As debt is repaid and the acquired business continues to grow at the EBITDA margins above 40% that it brought in, the return on that capital will improve faster than the headline ROIC figure currently suggests.

Capital Allocation and the Portfolio Signal

Few things communicate a management team's view of their own business more precisely than where they choose to deploy capital and what they decide to exit. Over the past four years, Standex has been consistent on both fronts. The acquisitions have targeted businesses with above-average EBITDA margins, exposure to secular growth end markets, and engineering differentiation that provides a basis for customer stickiness. The divestitures have targeted businesses at the opposite end of that spectrum.

The $462 million acquisition of Amran and Narayan in October 2024 was the capstone of a portfolio transformation that began well before that transaction. McStarlite, acquired in fiscal 2025, added aerospace structural components to Engineering Technologies and exposed the segment to commercial aviation production ramps with longer visibility than the general industrial markets it had historically served. Custom Biogenic Systems extended Scientific's reach in cryogenic storage with a product line carrying margins consistent with the segment's existing premium positioning. Sanyu Switch, acquired in fiscal 2024, added reed switch manufacturing in Japan, deepening the Electronics segment's geographic presence in a market where customer proximity matters for specification work.

Each transaction was small relative to the Amran and Narayan deal, but each followed the same logic: add businesses with engineering differentiation, above-average margins, and exposure to end markets where replacement cycles are long and the competitive entry barriers are real. The divestiture of Federal Industries in early 2026, a refrigerated display case business in the Specialty Solutions segment, confirms that the same logic runs in reverse. Management does not appear to be harvesting Federal Industries' cash flow, they are simplifying the portfolio to concentrate management attention and capital on the segments where the growth opportunity is most compelling.

Capital returns sit alongside growth investment in the hierarchy, not above it. The 246th consecutive quarterly dividend was declared in January 2026 at $0.34 per share, a 6.3% increase year-on-year and a continuation of an unbroken dividend payment record stretching back to the company's public listing in November 1964. The five-year dividend growth rate of approximately 7.6% suggests management views the current earnings trajectory as sufficiently durable to commit to progressive distributions even while carrying meaningful leverage from the Amran and Narayan transaction. The $76 million share repurchase program completed in the fiscal second quarter was additive to capital return but reflects opportunistic execution rather than a primary allocation priority at this leverage level.

The capacity investment currently underway is the most important forward-looking signal in the capital allocation picture. The new greenfield facility in Croatia serves European grid customers and is expected to reach $60 million in sales over three to five years, based on existing customer commitments. Additional manufacturing capacity in Mexico and Houston is being added specifically to support Grid demand. These are not speculative investments. They are responding to contracted customer requirements, which means the revenue they will eventually produce is partially visible already in the backlog and in the order activity that drove a book-to-bill above 1.0 for six consecutive quarters in Electronics.

Who Owns This and What It Signals

Barrow Hanley Mewhinney and Strauss holds the largest visible institutional stake, 393,000 shares representing 0.28% of its portfolio, with a value of approximately $85.4 million as of December 31, 2025. The average buy price of $138.80 reflects accumulation at levels well below where the stock trades today. For a fund of Barrow Hanley's orientation, quality-oriented, long-duration, value-disciplined, a holding of this size and vintage is not a passive outcome. It represents a view, held and renewed over multiple years, that the earnings power of this business was consistently underappreciated relative to the price being asked.

Driehaus Capital Management, with 86,600 shares and an average acquisition price of $195.71, tells a different story. Where Barrow Hanley built its position from a fraction of today's prices, Driehaus increased its holding by 61%, approximately 32,800 shares, at levels much closer to the current range. Driehaus is a growth-oriented manager with a documented history of positioning in businesses where earnings acceleration is underway but not yet fully reflected in valuations. Fresh capital added at prices well above the historical cost basis of longer-tenured holders suggests an updated assessment of the current trajectory, not a carried position from an earlier view.

Jane Street's 317% position increase, approximately 34,500 additional shares to bring the total to 45,300, at an average price of $204.69 is the most striking activity in the register. Jane Street is not a fundamental research shop; it is a market-making and arbitrage firm that builds positions where it has formed specific views on pricing inefficiencies. An increase of that magnitude at prices near the top of the historical range signals a specific and recent judgment about relative value. The timing coincides with the period when the Amran and Narayan acquisition's contribution to earnings first became clearly visible in quarterly filings.

Gamco Investors, with 75,100 shares at an average buy price of $60.26, represents the long-duration capital that has watched this transformation from the beginning. A modest 3.6% trim does not change the message: an investor who understood what this business was becoming early enough to buy at $60 saw something the market at large has taken considerably longer to recognize.

The collective picture is a shareholder base rotating from patient accumulation to active conviction. The old money built at low prices is largely still present and largely untrimmed. Fresh capital is arriving at higher prices from managers specifically pricing growth acceleration. That rotation is not noise. It is the institutional analogue of the organic growth inflection visible in the quarterly filings.

What the Multiple Is Not Yet Pricing

At roughly $250 per share and approximately 12.1 million shares outstanding, Standex carries a market capitalization of approximately $3.0 billion. Net debt of $437.7 million as of December 31, 2025 brings the enterprise value to approximately $3.45 billion. Against trailing twelve-month adjusted EBITDA of approximately $191 million, that implies an EV/adjusted EBITDA multiple of approximately 18 times. Against free cash flow, still compressed by the elevated capital expenditure program currently underway, the implied multiple sits near 50 times on a trailing basis, a figure that reflects investment in growth infrastructure rather than normalized earnings conversion.

The more useful metric for this specific business at this specific moment is the forward EV/EBITDA multiple once the capacity expansion normalizes. Management has guided to fiscal year 2026 revenue exceeding $900 million, with continued adjusted operating margin expansion. Using a conservative assumption of adjusted EBITDA margins near 22% on $900 million of revenue implies approximately $198 million of forward adjusted EBITDA, putting the forward EV/EBITDA at roughly 17.4 times on today's enterprise value. That figure is the appropriate starting point for a peer comparison.

AMETEK trades at 22.4 times trailing adjusted EBITDA with a ROIC of 12.5% and adjusted operating margins in the low-to-mid 20s. ITT trades at 18.1 times EBITDA with ROIC of 11.7% and adjusted operating margins near 22%. Curtiss-Wright, which operates across aerospace, defense, and nuclear power with adjusted operating margins near 19% and ROIC above 15%, trades at 33 times EBITDA and nearly 48 times FCF, the clearest illustration that the market is perfectly willing to pay substantial premiums for businesses with long-cycle defense and grid exposure when the revenue mix is legible. All three of these businesses are described by similar language: custom-engineered components, specification-driven customer relationships, long replacement cycles, and pricing power derived from technical differentiation rather than commodity volume.

Standex's Electronics segment fits that description precisely. The adjusted operating margin of 28.8% in the most recent quarter is not only above every peer shown above, it is approaching the margins that premium defense electronics businesses command. The difference is that Standex's company-level multiple is still shaped by the four smaller segments sitting alongside Electronics in the portfolio. That is both the explanation for the valuation gap and the source of the opportunity.

A simple sum-of-parts calculation makes the implied discount explicit. Non-Electronics segments contribute less than 40% of adjusted operating income across roughly half of company revenue, a profile consistent with 12 to 15 times EBITDA for businesses operating in mature, cyclical industrial end markets. Applying that range to the non-Electronics earnings base implies an enterprise value for those four segments of approximately $850 million to $1 billion. The remaining $2.4 to $2.6 billion represents the market's implied valuation of Electronics. Against Electronics adjusted EBITDA of approximately $120 million on a trailing basis, that implies a multiple of 20 to 22 times, below AMETEK's 22 times on lower margins and slower organic growth, and well below the premium that Curtiss-Wright's defense and grid-facing segments command. A business that earns more than its closest analog and grows faster than it has no structural reason to trade below it. That gap is the arithmetic of the opportunity.

The discount has three identifiable sources, each resolving on its own timeline. Portfolio complexity depresses the blended multiple: as long as four lower-margin segments sit alongside Electronics in the consolidated financials, the company-level EV/EBITDA trails the multiple that Electronics alone would command in isolation. That gap narrows organically as Electronics grows faster than the rest of the portfolio and closes further with each divestiture in non-core segments. Leverage of 2.3 times adjusted EBITDA, carried from the largest acquisition in the company's history, suppresses the multiple by a further turn or two relative to where a normalized balance sheet would trade; each quarter of debt repayment closes that gap. And Amran and Narayan, closed eighteen months ago, have not yet generated the earnings track record that institutional capital typically requires before assigning a structural re-rating. Six consecutive quarters of book-to-bill at or above 1.0 in Electronics is the beginning of that record. It is not the end.

The return from today's price resolves into three scenarios. In the base case, fiscal 2026 EBITDA of approximately $198 million against a flat 17 to 18 times forward multiple produces an enterprise value of $3.4 to $3.6 billion and, after net debt of $437.7 million, an implied equity value near $245 to $265 per share, roughly in line with today. That is the return of an owner who earns only earnings growth with no re-rating. In the upside case, a migration toward AMETEK's 22 times forward multiple on the same earnings base produces an enterprise value near $4.4 billion and equity value near $330 per share, a 32% return from today. That scenario does not require the market to become generous; it requires the market to value this segment's economics the way it already values AMETEK's, on lower margins and slower organic growth. In the downside case, if Grid demand disappoints materially and EBITDA falls to $170 million, a 15 times multiple produces an equity value near $180 per share, a 28% decline. That outcome requires both a demand shortfall and simultaneous multiple compression, a specific and identifiable scenario rather than a background risk.

The honest framing is that the base case preserves capital while earning modest earnings growth, the upside requires only recognition of economics the market already prices elsewhere, and the downside requires a specific negative confluence. The question this investment asks its owner is whether the Grid demand and new product contribution visible in the last six quarters is durable or front-loaded. The evidence, a book-to-bill above 1.0 for six consecutive quarters, capacity expansion funded by contracted commitments, and new product sales growing at 13% year-on-year, points toward durable. The next quarterly earnings, expected in early May, will deliver the first test of whether the third fiscal quarter continues that trajectory. Electronics organic growth, segment operating margin, and order flow are the three figures worth watching.

Final Thought

Standex International spent years building toward something. The portfolio pruning, the R&D investment, the new product cadence, the decision to write the largest check in the company's history for two Indian and American instrument transformer businesses, none of that was accidental. What the company looks like today, a business where the Electronics segment generates adjusted operating margins above 28% on revenue that is 52% of the total and growing at double-digit organic rates, is the intended destination of a multi-year capital allocation strategy that has been executed with unusual discipline.

Markets are slow to reprice transformations that happen inside historically unremarkable businesses. Standex spent long enough as a diversified industrial manufacturer that the label has proven sticky. The multiple still reflects that label more than it reflects the filings. For a long-term owner, that gap is the argument.

The electrical grid will require substantially more instrumentation, protection, and control infrastructure over the next decade than it has in any comparable period in modern history. The data centers driving AI infrastructure require cooling, power conditioning, and protection components that Standex now makes. Defense electronics demand is structurally elevated across every geography that matters to the company. These are not near-term catalysts. They are the demand conditions under which Standex's Grid and sensors businesses will continue to compound, quietly, in applications that rarely make headlines, at margins that most industrial businesses can only observe from a distance.

data.title: Wounded Secret Service Agent Was Shot in Chest, White House Says — WSJ
data.description:
By Alyssa Lukpat

The Secret Service agent wounded in the shooting at the White House Correspondents' Dinner was hit in the chest, White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said on Sunday. The agent "immediately moved to neutralize the shooter," she wrote on social media. The Secret Service said earlier that the officer who was shot was wearing a bullet-proof vest and has since been released from the hospital.

Leavitt added that the shooter "sought to assassinate the President and kill as many top Trump administration officials as possible."

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Italy to extradite suspected Chinese hacker wanted by US authorities, says source
data.description:
The Italian government has decided to extradite a Chinese man wanted by U.S. authorities on hacking charges that include stealing COVID-19 medical research, a person with direct knowledge of the matter told Reuters.

The government's decision, which was first reported by Bloomberg, follows a ruling by an Italian court earlier this month which said Xu Zewei could be extradited.

A representative for Italy's government declined to comment. Xu's lawyer Enrico Giarda told Reuters that his client had not received any communications on the matter so far.

Xu was arrested in Milan on July 3 at the request of U.S. authorities, who have accused him of wire fraud and aggravated identity theft for his alleged role in computer piracy acts that took place between February 2020 and June 2021.

Following his arrest, Xu's lawyer said his client had been a victim of mistaken identity.

The U.S. Justice Department (DOJ) alleges that Xu has been hacking and stealing crucial COVID-19 research at the behest of the Chinese government.

The DOJ has said that Xu was part of a team of cyber experts who in 2020 targeted U.S.-based universities, immunologists, and virologists conducting research into COVID‑19 vaccines, treatment, and testing.

The DOJ also alleges that in 2021, Xu was part of a cyber-espionage group known as Hafnium, which infiltrated thousands of computers worldwide, including in the U.S.

data.title: British Ambassador Says King's Visit is Still On — WSJ
data.description:
By Meridith McGraw

Christian Turner, the British ambassador to the U.S., said during a brunch on Sunday-one day after the White House Correspondents' Dinner shooting-that King Charles' upcoming visit to the U.S. would continue as scheduled.

"It's very British: Keep calm and carry on," Turner said at a brunch hosted by CNN. King Charles is set to arrive in Washington on Monday for a four-day visit that includes meetings with President Trump, a state dinner and an address to Congress.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Shooter's Written Statemet Discusses Security Breaches — WSJ
data.description:
By Natalie Andrews and Josh Dawsey

The statement from the suspected shooter at the Washington Hilton, viewed by The Wall Street Journal, outlines security breaches at the hotel that made getting firearms into the building easier than he expected.

Cole Allen, identified by authorities as the alleged gunman Saturday night, wrote of his experience traveling to Washington and getting into the hotel. "Like, I expected security cameras at every bend, bugged hotel rooms, armed agents every 10 feet, metal detectors out the wazoo," he wrote. "What I got (who knows, maybe they're pranking me!) is nothing." It is not clear when he wrote down the remarks.

Guests of events at the hotel were generally asked to show just a print-out of a ticket, and magnetometers were not used until just before entering the ballroom where the dinner was held.

Allen wrote that he took the lax security, which guests also noticed, as a "sense of arrogance." "I walk in with multiple weapons and not a single person there considers the possibility that I could be a threat," he wrote.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Suspected Shooter Describes Himself as 'Friendly Federal Assassin' in Writings — WSJ
data.description:
By Alyssa Lukpat and Josh Dawsey

The suspected shooter in Washington, D.C., called himself a "Friendly Federal Assassin" in writings authorities have tied to him. "I experience rage thinking about everything this administration has done," he wrote in a thousand-word document viewed by The Journal.

The suspect, Cole Allen, allegedly wrote that "doing something like this" felt awful. "I want to throw up," the document said. The author also expressed appreciation for people he said he likely wouldn't be able to talk to again, including his family, friends, students, colleagues and people he knew from church.

data.title: Correspondents' Association Board to Discuss Next Steps — WSJ
data.description:
By Natalie Andrews

Weijia Jiang, the president of the White House Correspondents' Association, issued a statement on Sunday morning, calling the shooting "a harrowing moment for everyone in attendance" at Saturday's dinner and expressing gratitude for U.S. Secret Service and all law-enforcement personnel. "We wish a full and speedy recovery to the officer who was injured in the line of duty," she wrote.

Trump has said that he wants to reschedule the dinner quickly. In her statement Jiang said that the association's board will meet to "assess what happened and determine how to proceed. We will provide updates as soon as any are available."

data.title: Bitcoin Leverage Builds as Price Stalls Below $80,000
data.description:
Bitcoin traders are stacking the long side of futures by more than three to one, according to Coinglass. The skew points to bullish conviction near $77,500 but raises the threat of forced selling on a sharp pullback.

The lopsided positioning led to open interest in BTC perpetuals sliding roughly 6% to 744,300 BTC over 24 hours. Traders are starting to trim leverage, but long bias still holds across major venues.

Long Bias Meets a Stalling Spot Price

Bitcoin failed to clear $80,000 earlier this week and has since drifted toward $77,500, according to Yahoo Finance. That stall has done little to shake long-side conviction. The long/short ratio on Coinglass still shows more than 3 longs per short.

History shows that extreme imbalances often precede contrarian moves. Crowded one-sided trades become easy fuel for short-term reversals.

Coinglass logged $22.44 million in long liquidations on April 25 against $11.60 million on the short side. The roughly two-to-one wipeout hints bulls are absorbing more pain even as account-level positioning stays heavily long.

Bitcoin Liquidation Map Flags Concentrated Risk Pockets

The Coinglass map shows dense clusters of leveraged long positions stacked beneath the current spot price. The arrangement historically amplifies downside moves through cascading liquidations.

Each liquidated long adds market sell flow that can push the price into the next cluster.

Earlier in April, $71 million in long positions sat at risk under $77,300. Above $78,000, short-squeeze conditions fueled a sweep that wiped out millions in bearish bets. Rising leverage and open interest have repeatedly preceded sharp corrections this cycle.

Whether spot defends $77,000 may decide if the next move is a controlled cool-off or a sharper liquidation cascade. For now, the imbalance leaves the market structurally fragile despite the bullish optics.
